text: sharing a case of a robotic tap for an incisional hernia. This is a supraumbilical hernia that was created after a robotic prostatectomy. Patient says that when they woke up from surgery, there was already a hernia there. This is several months after that operation and I chose to do this as a robotic tap, not wanting to go into the retroactive space yet and still trying to keep mesh out of the abdomen. You can see I was dealing with some very thin peritoneum at the start. If you struggle through this a little bit, you can get the flap to get going. The other thing that made this challenging was that he had lack of abdominal wall fat plane, that midline fat plane that usually extends laterally a little bit. But if you just take your time, you can usually get that flap started. And as you get closer to the midline, it just gets easier and easier. So I'm just trying to make a large wide flap here, crossing over the midline above. And then later you'll see me do this below the hernia, which lets you kind of attack the hernia directly with that volcano sign that someone likes to tell us about all the time.

The surgeon attempts to preserve the peritoneum from the hernia sac but encounters difficulty due to its fusion with the sac. A large defect is created in the hernia sac, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the peritoneal flap laterally, extending the dissection as far as possible.

The surgeon dissects down the transversalis muscle towards the retroperitoneum, aiming to extend the flap across the midline to the ipsilateral side. The hernia defect measures approximately four centimeters wide and three centimeters long. The defect is closed transversely, with the mesh extending beyond the ipsilateral flap creation to ensure complete coverage. The flap is dissected laterally to prevent mesh exposure, and the closure is achieved without tension, with pressure reduced to eight.

The surgeon closes the defect in the peritoneal flap transversely, ensuring it extends across the ipsilateral side. A 15x15 mesh is placed over the hernia defect, with a 3-0 silk suture tacked at the center to secure it. Additional tacks are placed around the corners of the mesh to hold it in position.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap, extending it past the lateral corner of the mesh on the ipsilateral side to ensure complete coverage. The mesh is not entirely inside the peritoneal defect, but the flap covers it without tearing. Any remaining holes are closed separately with 3-0 Vicryl sutures.
